To say we do not (cannot) know things-in-themselves is as much a Transcendental Illusion as to say we do.69 All that Cavell’s argument amounts to thus far is the idea that we don’t understand what the skeptic says, along with the implied suspicion that neither does the skeptic. Arriving at a place where a purported claim turns up as empty is not a refutation of anything. Nor is it a demonstration that, as a matter of general philosophical principle, one can’t say what the skeptic is trying to say. This is enough to make clear that there is something seriously wrong with the idea that Cavell believes he has given a conclusive therapeutic refutation of skepticism. IV e see, then, that Cavell thinks one reason why material object (external world) skepticism “cannot” be refuted is quite simply that it doesn’t really provide anything to refute. One could, I suppose, construe this as a kind of logical or philosophical sense in which he imagines anti-skeptical arguments as missing their targets. Yet according to Cavell, this is hardly the only or even the deepest sense in which such arguments are unsatisfactory. To see this, consider that one could well wonder why it is that skepticism arises in the first place. Or better, once having been exposed as incoherent by a diagnosis of the type Cavell offers, one could well ask why skepticism doesn’t merely evaporate or disappear, as certain illusions or bad ideas sometimes do, never to return. Cavell holds, on the other hand, that skepticism is a pervasive problem in everyday life and an accordingly a recurring theme in philosophy, film, and drama. hat, in his view, accounts for this phenomenon? e first need to note that since skepticism as Cavell understands it is inherently tied to the constant vulnerability of our criteria to failure and rejection, it would never arise for a creature without language. ithout meaning to imply that he would ever suggest that a creature-with-language could be intelligibly factored into parts in anything but a nominal sense, Cavell would I believe allow for one or the other aspect of this unified whole to be emphasized in a given case, especially if doing so permitted one to make a salient point. In the present case we can say that for Cavell, skepticism is not only part and parcel of our being creatures-with-language (and minds), but perhaps more pertinently creatures-with-language and minds.70 This makes clearer why he regards theoretical attacks intended to refute skepticism as even more profoundly mis-calibrated than we have seen thus far; they fail to take into account how deeply skepticism pertains to our affective nature as language users, however much it can appear in philosophy to be a primarily if not wholly speculative problem. At the most basic level in his thinking, therefore, Cavell holds that attempts to refute skepticism misfire because the impulse to skepticism is, qua impulse, not at root an intellectual position and so not a “suitable object of refutation”. Skepticism’s manifestation as a philosophical problem is more aptly seen for him as a kind of (as we’ll see in a moment, motivated) distortion or sublimation of the genuine vulnerabilities of an animal with language into an inflated intellectual problem. In The Claim of Reason, Cavell is referring precisely to such covering up of the affective roots of philosophical skepticism when he recalls “what I have throughout kept arriving at as the cause of skepticism – the attempt to convert the human condition, the condition of humanity, into an intellectual difficulty, a riddle.”71 The idea that human speakers face a permanent susceptibility to skepticism traces out a remarkably consistent Leitfaden running through Cavell’s authorship over more than 40 years. Consider first this famous passage from his ground-breaking essay on ittgenstein from 1962: For ittgenstein, philosophy comes to grief not in denying what we all know to be true, but in its effort to escape those human forms of life which alone provide the coherence of our expression. He wishes acknowledgement of human limitation which does not leave us chafed by our own skin, by a sense of powerlessness to penetrate beyond the human conditions of knowledge.72 In his magnum opus from 1979 we read, If the fact that we share, or have established, criteria is the condition under which we can think and communicate in language, then skepticism is a natural possibility of that condition; it reveals most perfectly the standing threat to thought and communication, that they are only human, nothing more than natural to us.73 Some years later, in an essay from the mid-1980s, Cavell describes the possibilities of skepticism as “natural to the human being; parts of the nature or fate of a creature complex enough for, or fated to, language.74 In works from the mid and late 1990s, Cavell asserts for example that the ordinary language philosopher’s sense of returning words from their metaphysical to their everyday use is driven by a sense of a human dissatisfaction with words (not as it were solely a philosophical dissatisfaction) in which an effort to transcend or to purify speech ends by depriving the human speaker of a voice in what becomes his (or, differently, her) fantasy of knowledge, a characterization I have given of what happens in skepticism.75 He makes substantially the same claim in an essay published a couple of years later: Reading tragedy back into philosophical skepticism I would variously, in various connections, characterize the skeptic as craving the emptiness of language, as ridding himself of the responsibilities of meaning, and as being drawn to annihilate externality or otherness, projects I occasionally summarize as seeking to escape the conditions of humanity, which I call the chronic human desire to achieve the inhuman, the monstrous, from above or from below.76 Finally, in a late essay from the 2000s, Cavell is still articulating this perspective, for instance in maintaining that the question of what I might call skepticism’s necessity and its possibility, to its paradoxical presence within our very possession of language, glimpsed when Descartes asserts that we are misled by the ordinary word “see” into supposing that we really as it were see things of the world.77 Indeed, Cavell consistently holds in addition that this tendency to deny the human by attempting to evade the burdens of human speech and thought is an essential possibility that goes into the very makeup of the concept of the human being itself. Passages such as these, written over many years, illustrate this point: (T)he denial of the human, the wish to escape the conditions of humanity, call them conditions of finitude, is itself only human.78 Nothing is more human than the wish to deny one’s humanity, or to assert it at the expense of others.79 … the denial of the human is the human; thinking to escape human nature from above, and from below … to interpret a metaphysical finitude as an intellectual lack.80 … skepticism’s negation of the human, its denial of satisfaction in the human (here in human conditions of knowing), is an essential feature of the human, as it were its birthright.81 … a final philosophical victory over (the temptation) to skepticism, which would mean a victory over the human …82 … I persist in thinking that to lose knowledge of the human possibility of skepticism means to lose knowledge of the human …83 Further, the relation to skepticism that Cavell regards as endemic to the life of language users does not in his eyes remain a bare, even if constant, unactualized possibility. As I have already pointed out in the introduction, on occasion Cavell makes the even stronger claim that we are fated to full-blown outbreaks of skeptical madness. Here are two passages cited previously: hat turns it upon us is philosophy, the desire for thought, running out of control. That has become an inescapable fate for us, apparently accompanying the fate of having human language.84 I mean to say that it is human, it is the human drive to transcend itself, make itself inhuman, which should not end until, as in Nietzsche, the human is over.85 These sorts of remarks are not isolated or idiosyncratic; other passages make it clear that Cavell views skepticism as not merely a perhaps unfortunate, but ultimately manageable impulse. His use of terms like “drive” and related terms warrants a reading in which he thinks it is simply part of our nature that we will in fact find ourselves overcome by unbridled fits of irrational intellectual aggression: I take skepticism not as the moral of a cautious science laboring to bring light into a superstitious, fanatical world, but as the recoil of demonic reason, irrationally thinking to dominate the earth. I take it to begin as a wish not to reject the world but rather to establish it.86 The human capacity – and the drive – both to affirm and to deny our criteria constitutes the argument of the ordinary. And to trace the disappointment with criteria is to trace the aspiration to the sublime – the image of the skeptic’s progress.87 As we have already seen, on Cavell’s analysis the skeptic’s argument requires a non-claim context. In so far as the traditional anti-skeptic tries to defeat the skeptic, he acquiesces in this initial yet “decisive conjuring trick”. Both the skeptic and the anti-skeptic accept the intelligibility of a picture of language where the criteria that guide our words and concepts are self-applying, operating in a vacuum, isolated from the contextual factors that make it understandable why someone some place at some time might be concerned to say what she says at all. Moreover, Cavell thinks that such denial of the conditions for how language, mind, and world might come together is not surprising. On the contrary, such blind ambition is bound to break out. It is Cavell’s fascination with our inability to live with the all of the contingency, complicity and complexity that comes with our application of our criteria to the world and to each other, that motivates his work on philosophy, literature, and film.88 But all of this only begs the question as to what kind of view of the human being underlies Cavell’s view of skepticism’s inevitability. hy, in fact, is he so confident that skeptical worries will return to haunt us? This is a central question for this essay. In order to begin our approach to it, we can take another rightfully celebrated quotation from Cavell’s early essay on the later ittgenstein. e learn and we teach certain words in certain contexts, and then we are expected, and expect others, to be able to project them into further contexts. Nothing insures that this projection will take place (in particular, not the grasping of universals nor the grasping of books of rules), just as nothing insures that we will make, and understand, the same projections. That on the whole we do is a matter of our sharing routes of interest and feeling, modes of response, senses of humor and of significance and of fulfillment, of what is outrageous, of what is similar to what else, what a rebuke, what forgiveness, of when an utterance is an assertion, when an appeal, when an explanation – all the whirl of organism ittgenstein calls ‘forms of life.’ Human speech and activity, sanity and community, rest upon nothing more, but nothing less, than this. It is a vision as simple as it is difficult, and as difficult as it is (and because it is) terrifying.89 Because criteria don’t just apply themselves on their own, but require our involvement, nothing guarantees that we will make the effort to apply them, let alone that we will apply them correctly and successfully. As we shall see shortly, moreover, Cavell believes there is much that militates against our willingness to own up to precisely these possibilities. In fact, after The Claim of Reason, it was the investigation of this theme in philosophy and various art forms that came to dominate Cavell’s energy even more than it had before that watershed publication. Now we saw earlier that Cavell wants to say that there is a “truth of skepticism” to which the skeptic has latched on, even if not always in a very perspicuous manner. As Cavell puts it, this truth was that “Our relations to the world as a whole, or to others in general, is not one of knowing, where knowing construes itself as being certain”.90 The basis for what I think of as Cavell’s “anthropology” resides precisely in what he finds to be our human inability, both in our relations to the world and to each other, to live with the conditions that underlie this truth.91 In Cavell’s various numerous elaborations of skepticism, it is clear that he believes that we find living with this truth to be an almost unbearable task. Hence, his repeated claims that it is human to deny the human condition. But why do we find facing this condition so hard? hy is the contingency internal to criteria so terrifying that Cavell finds we are driven almost of necessity to deny their very existence? Consider in this vein two long quotations taken from the same stretch of The Claim of Reason that vividly portray the core of a Cavellian response to these questions. My major claim about the philosopher’s originating question – e.g., “(How) do (can) we know anything about the world?” or “hat is knowledge; what does my knowledge of the world consist in?” – is that it … is a response to, or expression of, a real experience which takes hold of human beings. It is not “natural” in the sense I have already found in the claim to “reasonableness”: it is not a response to questions raised in ordinary practical contexts, framed in language which any master of a language will accept as ordinary. But it is, as I might put it, a response which expresses a natural experience of a creature complicated or burdened enough to possess language at all. hat experience? ell, of course, an experience or sense that one may know nothing about the real world. But what kind of experience is that? How or when does it emerge?92 The experience of knowing nothing about the real world at all is of course the experience of the total failure of our criteria to make actual contact with it. A bit further on we find the following: I can here only attest to my having had such experiences and, though struggling against them intellectually, have had to wait for them to dissipate in their own time. It seems to me that I relive such experiences when I ask my students, as habitually at the beginning of a course in which epistemology is discussed, whether they have ever had such thoughts as, for example, that they might, when for all the world awake, be dreaming; or that if our senses, for example our eyes, had differently evolved, we would sense, i.e., see, things other than as we see them now, so that the way we see them now is almost accidental, anyway at least as dependent on our constitution as on the constitution of the world itself; or that the things of the world would seem just as they now do to us if there were nothing in it but some power large enough either to keep us in a sort of hypnotic spell, or to arrange the world for our actions as a kind of endless stage-set, whose workings we can never get behind, for after all consider how little of anything, or any situation, we really see. I know well enough, intellectually as it were, that these suppositions may be nonsense, seem absurd, when raised as scruples about particular claims to knowledge. But if these experiences have worked in the initial motivation of particular claims, then the attempt to prove intellectually that they have no sense is apt to weaken one’s faith in intellectuality… . hen the experience created by such thoughts is there, it is something that presents itself to me as one, as I have wished to express it, of being sealed off from the world, enclosed within my own endless succession of experiences. It is an experience for which there must be a psychological explanation; but no such explanation would or should prove its epistemological insignificance. The iniial focus of my discussion is Rane Willerslev’s Soul Huners: Huning, Animism, and Personhood among he Siberian Yukaghirs from 2007.131 The Yukaghirs are a people of he Upper Kolyma River in norheasern Siberia, and Willerslev’s book is an exploraion of aspecs of he huning pracices of Yukaghir culure, in paricular of he Yukaghir undersanding of he naure and role of he self in hese pracices. Moreover, is discussion of he Yukaghir undersanding of personhood suggess fruiful comparisons wih oher ehnographic maerial ha I will menion, if only cursorily. There are several key ideas ha make up Willerslev’s porrai of he Yukaghirs. The firs is animism, which he inroduces ino his discussion wih some cauion. For now, i is enough o noe ha Willerslev quie sensibly mainains agains his kind of dogmaism ha one canno even ge so far so as a criique of a people’s pracices and conceps unless one akes he ime o undersand wha hose pracices and conceps enail. Naurally, his begins, a he very leas, wih aking seriously heir self-inerpreaions when i comes o heir own lives.133 As Willerslev pus i, For us in he Wes, i is cusomary o assume ha aribues of personhood, wih all his enails in erms of language, inenionaliy, reasoning, and moral awareness, belong exclusively o human beings. Animals are undersood o be wholly naural beings, and heir behavior is ypically explained as auomaic and insincive. Among he Yukaghirs, however, a differen assumpion prevails. In heir world, persons can ake a variey of forms, of which a human being is only one… . Moreover, humans and animals can move in and ou of differen species’ perspecives by emporarily aking on each oher’s bodies. Indeed, among he Yukaghirs, as we shall see laer, his capaciy o ake on he appearance and viewpoin of anoher being is one of he key aspecs of being a person.134 However, grasping he conen of hese ideas sill may no be adequae. Any meaningful comparison beween sysems of hough furher requires ha one correcly see, even if one does no accep, he role ha hese ideaional srucures play in he lives one wishes o undersand. And, as Willerslev underscores in his regard, animism among he Yukaghirs is no an explicily ariculaed docrinal sysem.135 Insead, “he personhood of animals and things is, as we shall see, something that emerges in particular contexts of close practical involvement, such as during hunting”.136 Consequently, for the Yukaghirs, animals and other nonhumans are conceived as persons, not because personhood has been bestowed upon them by some kind of cognitive processing, but because they reveal themselves as such within relational contexts of real-life activities, such as during hunting.137 Complementing animism is another key notion for Willerslev’s account of the Yukaghirs, namely mimesis. He describes the Yukaghir cosmos as “in effect a hall of mirrors, as various dimensions of reality are conceived as replicas or reflections of others” and adds that In sum, the world of the Yukaghirs is by and large a “mimeticized” world: everything is paired with an almost endless number of mimetic doubles of itself, which extend in all directions and continually mirror and echo one another.138 Mimesis becomes especially prominent when it comes to hunting: Animals and their associated spiritual beings are … said to take on human shapes and live lives analogous to those of humans when in their own lands and households. Likewise, when the hunter seeks to bring an elk out into the open by mimicking its bodily movements, he is inevitably put into a paradoxical situation of mutual mimicry. As a result, the bodies of the two blend to a point that makes them of the same kind.139 The Yukaghir hunter not only understands himself as taking on the perspective of the person who is his prey, but also as a reincarnation of a deceased relative: The Yukaghir hunter assumes the viewpoint, senses, and sensibilities of his prey while still remaining aware of himself as a human hunter with the intention of killing it. Likewise, the Yukaghir person is intertwined with the deceased relative of whom he is considered a reincarnation sharing the same name, personality and knowledge. Yet he also retains a personhood of his own and is capable of individual agency and intention.140 More generally, according to the Yukaghirs’ notion of reincarnation, each living person is seen as a kind of new embodiment of a particular dead relative. In fact, everyone in Nelemnoye would always insist that the living person is the dead relative of whom he or she is a reincarnation. “The two are one and the same person,” they assured me. Yet some people would add, “but a person’s body is also him or herself,” thereby illustrating that it is an aspect of the person, his ayibii or soul, rather than the person in toto that is reincarnated. An individual’s body is, in principle, himself or herself, but, as we shall see below, it can at times be “colonized” by the ayibii, which might divide itself into its various parts and take control.141 In fact, the relation between the “empirical person” or embodied individual and the reincarnated ayibii is such that “the perspective of the dead person he claims to be can sometimes penetrate his own perspective”142 This means that at a basic level the empirical individual “is neither himself nor his dead relative in any absolute sense, but is something in between or both at once. The implication of this, however, involves not only perceptual experience, but the very structures of embodiment as well”.143 These brief considerations on animism, mimesis, and reincarnation naturally raise questions about how the Yukaghirs understand the just mentioned concept of the soul, or ayibii. The word “ayibii” is translated as “shadow” in Yukaghir, something that might suggest a dualism of body and spirit.144 Willerslev points out, however that Among the Yukaghirs, however, souls do not have this disembodied existence but are vested with a good deal of physicality. We need only point out that the hunter occasionally sees his ayibii in dreams as a freely operating being, and in waking life he sees his own soul or that of his fellows in the shape of an animal.145 Willerslev also cites work suggesting that, despite conflicting testimony, many Yukaghir believe each individual has three ayibii, the “head soul” representing the intellect, the “heart soul” controlling motion, and a third soul pervading the body and controlling physiological functions.146 The heart, head, and shadow are among its favorite locations, which is why people tend to talk about three souls, the head-ayibii, the heart-ayibii, and the shadow. However, the ayibii can, in principle, reside in all body parts and organs. Diffused throughout the body in this way, the ayibii becomes individualized into different agents or “persons” (Rus, lyudi), as he called tem, eac of wic takes its specific caracter from te body part or organ it inabits… . Tus, eac of tese animated body parts or organs is understood to be a kind of person witin te person. However, as e empasized, te ayibii is originally only one, and it can terefore gater itself into woleness and act as a single person.147 As wit animism, Willerslev notes ow tere is a lack of an explicit teoretical account of ow tese various aspects ang togeter: “In general, Yukagirs sowed little interest in my questions about te number of souls and were in te person’s body tey migt possibly dwell.148 As wit animism, Willerslev underscores ow te Yukagir understanding of ayibii comes out best in te practical contexts were it is relevant; tere is little effort given to reconciling wat is said in tese practical contexts in abstraction from tem.149 Willerslev empasizes, owever, tat none of tis sould be taken to imply tat te Yukagirs subscribe to some version of monism in wic everyting is understood as simply a part or aspect of an all-encompassing “over-self”. On te contrary, e makes it clear for te Yukagir, everyday practical life demands a kind of “dept reflexivity” as a form of defense mecanism against te dissolution of te self, wic faces a real risk tat identification wit te world of oter bodies, tings, and people will become so complete tat all te differences will appear to vanis and an irreversible metamorposis will occur.150 Te risk of suc self-dissolution is nowere more manifest tan in te context of unting: We must bear in mind tat in te world of te Yukagirs, were te various boundaries between self and oter, suc as te ones between living and dead or uman and animal, are permeable and easily crossed, te ability not to confuse analogy wit identity is of paramount importance. Tis is probably most clearly revealed wit regard to te Yukagir unter and is prey… . (H)unters consider it necessary to assume te identity of teir prey in order to kill it. However, if te unter loses sigt of is own uman self in tis process and surrenders to te single perspective of te animal, e will undergo an irreversible metamorposis and transform into te animal imitated. In tis case, ten, confusion between analogy and identity does not lead to madness as suc, but instead to someting just as dreadful, namely “otering” beyond recovery.151 I now want to touc briefly on some interesting resonances tat Willerslev notes between is account of te Yukagirs’ understanding of personood and oter descriptions in te literature. First, Willerslev suggests a connection between te Yukagir understanding and te Amerindian in te work of Eduardo Viveiros de Castro. Recall tat we read previously tat according to Willerslev, “Animals and teir associated spiritual beings are … said to take on uman sapes and live lives analogous to tose of umans wen in teir own lands and ouseolds”. Later e adds tat, “Tis is wat Viveiros de Castro … calls ‘perspectivism’ ”, namely, te conception “according to wic te world is inabited by different kinds of persons, uman and non-uman, wic perceive reality from distinct points of view”.152 Like Willerslev, Viveiros de Castro does not sy away from using te term “animism” in is etnograpic work: Wat I am defining ere is wat antropologists of yore used to call animism, an attitude tat is far more tan an idle metapysical tenet, for te attribution of souls to animals and oter so-called natural beings entails a specific way of dealing wit tem.153 As Viveiros de Castro glosses it, Amerindian animism as some profound consequences: Personification or subjectification implies tat te “intentional stance” adopted wit respect to te world as been in some way universalized. Instead of reducing intentionality to obtain a perfectly objective picture of te world, animism makes te inverse epistemological bet.154 Elsewere e claims tat tis view entails a “cultural universalism” and correlative “multinaturalism”. Humans sare a commonly structured person-saped understanding wit oter species, and eac species lives in an equally real natural world saped by its specific perceptual needs.155 More striking still is te similarity in Willerslev’s and Viveiros de Castro’s respective descriptions of te dangers wic suc animism present for te Yukagirs and Amerindians. Willerslev writes, If … mimesis becomes totalizing, and te imitator loses imself in wat e imitates, we are no longer talking about mimesis but metamorposis: noting is left to imitate wen te difference between te copy and te original is totally gone. Mimesis, terefore, is situated and defined troug difference as muc as troug similarity.156 Consider in tis vein tis passage from Viveiros de Castro in wic e compares te treat of solipsism implicit wit Western objectivism wit an opposite and peraps even more terrifying possibility inerent in Amerindian views: Solipsism and metamorposis are related in te same way. Solipsism is te pantom tat treatens our cosmology, raising te fear tat we will not recognize ourselves in our “own kind” because, given te potentially absolute singularity of minds, our “own kind” are actually not like us. Te possibility of metamorposis expresses te fear – te opposite fear – of no longer being able to differentiate between uman and animal, and above all te fear of seeing te uman wo lurks witin te body of te animal tat one eats. Our traditional problem in te West is ow to connect and universalize: individual substances are given, wile relations ave to be made. Te Amerindian problem is ow to separate and particularize: relations are given, wile substances must be defined.157 Willerslev also indicates affinities between is depiction of personood among te Yukagir and Marilyn Stratern’s work in Melanesia, were se developed te idea of a “dividual”.158 Stratern writes, Melanesian persons are as dividually as tey are individually conceived. Tey contain a generalized sociality witin. Indeed, persons are frequently constructed as te plural and composite site of te relationsips tat produced tem. Te singular person can be imagined as a social microcosm.159 Wen writing later tat one “of te best known Melansian axioms must be tat appearances deceive”, se adds tat “te unitary identity sets te stage for te revelation tat it covers or contains witin itself oter identities”.160 Stratern goes on to sow ow tis Melanesian imagining of te person as a social microcosm breaks profoundly wit a dominant Western understanding of eac person’s relations to er body, er actions, er property, and oters. According to Stratern, on te latter conception, tese relations are construed on te model of ownersip. Se tus writes, Western proprietism ineres in te way relations between persons and tings are conceived troug te metapor of te commodity. Persons are assumed to be te proprietors of teir persons (including teir own will, teir energies, and work in te general sense of directed activity).161 Naturally, suc a view entails tat oter persons, let alone external objects, are conceptually extraneous to one’s identity. Wile tis may be common sense for many of us, Stratern counters tat te Western notion tat people own wat tey do to te exclusion of oters seems ardly borne out by te evidence on ow claims and ceremonial duties in suc non-Western societies are often structured… . To assume tat one can take as an analytical base a condition in wic people control temselves and teir labor, witout reference to social oters, is to introduce a neoclassical economism wen one least expects it.162 In sarp contrast wit tis “proprietary” paradigm, Stratern maintains tat te Melanesian cases delineate te impact wic interaction as on te inner person. Te body’s features are a register, a site of tat interaction. Consequently, wat is drawn out of te person are te social relationsips of wic it is composed: it is a microcosm of relations.163 Consequently, tere “is no presumption of an innate unity: suc an identity is only created to special, transient effect”.164 Te situation in wic a transient unitary identity is created, or drawn out, is one in wic a specific action is called for by someone else or someting else to wic a person bears te appropriate kind of relation. Action ere is terefore not regarded as an effect of a unitary willing spiritual entity. Rater, it is a temporal manifestation of just one of many relations arbored witin eac dividual person. Stratern tus distinguises between a person, a nest of relations as it were, and an agent, a single actor activated on a given occasion by an external entity, be tat anoter person or object.165 Se writes, Te person is constituted from te vantage points of te relations tat constitute im or er; se or e objectifies and is tus revealed in tose relations. Te agent is construed as te one wo acts because of tose relationsips and is revealed in is or er actions.166 An agent’s action is tus instigated by anoter person or object; it is te presence of tese tat calls for some particular response because of te way in wic tey too are understood as sets of social relations. Tere are no clear nodal points of subjects acting on objects (weter tose objects are understood as oter persons or ordinary objects suc as food stuffs). Instead, Te wole burden of analyzing te objectifications of a gift economy as been tat, if we use te sortand at all, it is persons (relations) wo replace persons (relations). Tese may appear as bodies, but ten te body is an objectified form: te self so to speak from anoter’s point of view.167 Stratern sums up tis radically different way in wic persons, agents, teir objects, and teir relations are understood in te Melanesian context as se finds it. Te center is were te twentiet-century Western imagination puts te self, te personality, te ego. For te ‘person’ in tis latter day Western view is an agent, a subject, te autor of tougt and action and tus ‘at te center or relationsips… . It as saped our cultural obsession wit te extent to wic uman subjects are actors wo create relationsips or act rater as te precipitation of relationsips; tis obsession fuels te individual/society dicotomy wit wic I began.168 Despite tese notable similarities, owever, tere also seem to be some interesting differences between te Hagen and Yukagir. So far as I can tell from Willerslev’s account, personood among te Yukagir can involve a transitory multiple identity of te unter, is prey, and a deceased relative. Tis involves not only cross-species relations, but also bot syncronic and diacronic uman relations. Te main focus in Stratern’s description of te Hagen, on te oter and, seems to me to be on syncronic social relations alone.169 It’s tus unclear to me weter it would be apt to say of tem, as Willerslev does of te Yukagirs (and peraps would say of te Amerindian in Viveiros de Castro’s work) tat tey live in a “all of mirrors”. Finally, I sould repeat tat te understandings of personood in te etnograpic material described previously certainly seems to be rater different from Taylor’s account of te medieval porous self. In tese etnograpic descriptions of non-buffered selves, rater tan a porous, yet mostly discreet, individual mind or soul existing in bot direct causal and semantic contact wit entities in te world, we find someting more like te attainment of wolesale multiple identities in one and te same embodied being. So far as I can see, ten, even if a person is not exactly understood as bound at te skin, as in te modern Western picture, te specific notion of porosity seems eiter to be absent in te summarized descriptions, or at least not to be in te forefront of tem.170 VII Tere is no getting around te fact tat te descriptions of te people recounted in te previous section are puzzling (even toug tey are often te very stuff of wic muc istory and etnograpy is made). One peraps all too uman response to tis perplexity is to try to explain it away. Willerslev notes for instance an aversion in some quarters to ascribing even self-proclaimed animist views to peoples on te grounds tat it smacks of “primitivism”. Some writers ave actually preferred to treat certain paradoxical statements made by informants as deceptions offered up in order to conceal te real nature of teir activities from overly inquisitive Western investigators.171 I completely agree wit Marsall Salin’s assessment tat tis option fails miserably as an attempt to sow respect to indigenous cultures. Rater, it does “symbolic violence” to tem and sacrifices uge portions of etnograpy to a “moral sakedown” imposed by a misguided political agenda.172 It simply strains credulity to believe tat te Yukagir unters wit wom Willerslev lived, and te informants of countless oter etnograpers wo ave similarly investigated oter cultures, were putting on an elaborate sow to satisfy is need to project a Eurocentric fantasy onto an exotic “oter”. Tis is just political umbug. At any rate, my main conclusion is tat tere is no good reason for attributing to tese people an essential relation to skepticism as Cavell conceives of it. In fact I find it difficult, to put it mildly, to accommodate Cavell’s vision of skepticism, tied as it is to te experience of te locked-in/locked-out self, witin te contexts of tese oter cultures. On te contrary, in te cultural-istorical worldviews we ave been looking at, tere seems to be little or no place for te very experience of a fundamental predicament of separateness. Indeed, te ideas of “acknowledging te oter” or “accepting te world” seem forced in tese contexts, since te oters and te world are already very muc woven into te fabric of te self. Of course it’s important to keep in mind tat on Cavell’s view, tat eac of us is is “own aecceity” is not a conclusion we arrive at troug pilosopical argumentation, in te manner in wic, for example, Descartes seeks to establis tis kind of proposition after is First Meditation. Even if e seems to tink tat Descartes was on to someting significant, for Cavell te pilosopical doctrine of our isolation only seems to result if we follow te misguided assumption tat our fundamental relation to te world migt ave been one of knowing wit certainty. If, as I tink Cavell rigtly argues, tere is someting fundamentally illusory wit tis assumption anyway, ten tis fact or proposition, if tose are te rigt words, is not someting we could ever really know. Instead, te “experience” leading to te “pilosoper’s originating question”, an experience Cavell tinks we usually respond to by fleeing into various obsessions, including various epistemological projects, is supposed to be a revealing mood tat uncovers our true predicament of separateness, even if tat revelation is not construed by Cavell as a cognitive operation and tat separateness is at least temporarily remediable in a way tat pilosopical solipsism never could be. Our condition of separateness as to be lived wit and faced up to, not just cognized, if we are to attain te only genuine relations to oters and te world of wic we are capable, te relations of acknowledgement and acceptance. Still, even during tose brief respites wen te dust of our panic settles and we are peraps able, owever fleetingly, to accept te world and to acknowledge oters before we once again succumb to our mania for domination troug knowledge, Cavell’s view is tat our fundamental existential condition must remain tat of separateness. In te context of tese oter systems of tougt, owever, te for us apparently redeeming concepts of acknowledgement and acceptance seem frankly redundant and out of place. In fact, not only does it become difficult to talk about any possible edifying lesson to be ad troug aving te experiences of separateness described so vividly by Cavell, I tink it is completely opaque ow, for instance, we can realistically entertain te question as to wat it would be like for te Yukagir to undergo suc an experience. Yet according to Cavell, it was te essence of our very umanity to be vulnerable precisely to te feeling of te world’s witdrawal, if only long enoug to repress it. I don’t buy it. It could be argued tat I am defending an extremely implausible form of psycological relativism, to be denying te “psycic unity of mankind”, by implying tat all emotional experience is determined by language, and, were tere seem to be different grammars of suc experience, concluding tat tere we ave logically incompatible emotional possibilities. Noting is furter from te case. I take it as given tat joy, fear, grief, anger, and expectation are part of our common natural ineritance and are found universally in all cultures. But experiences suc as tat of “being sealed off from te world, enclosed witin my own endless succession of experiences”173 or of oneself being under treat of metamorposing into a moose seem to me only possible for a creature burdened wit a language. And it seems furter to me to be not only te case tat certain tings are simply not given expression in te context of certain forms of life, but entirely unclear ow to conceive of teir sensibly being given suc expression. At tis point, I migt be accused of trying to get away wit a logical sleigt of and. Tat is, one migt try to sow tat my argument against Cavell’s claim for te universality of te uman condition of separateness relies on noting more tan a sopworn and now generally rejected argument from incommensurability of te kind commonly associated wit figures suc as Tomas Kun, Peter Winc, and oters.174 In te examples from istory and etnograpy I ave discussed, so tis counterargument could go, certain words suc as “self”, “person”, “agent”, “soul”, “mind”, “individual”, “I”, “body”, and “world” appear in very different linguistic contexts and seem to be employed in very different ways tan tose ways to wic most of us in te modern West are familiar. If, moreover, tese lexical items, tese signs, ave watever particular meaning tey ave in tose very different contexts, ow, it can be asked, can I pretend to locate in tese oter contexts of use wat Cavell means by te same expressions in te contexts of use in wic e employs tese terms? Tis would require tat I understand wat Cavell means by tem in is context, and ten, wen turning my attention to te appearance of tese expressions in etnograpic contexts, I am someow able to recognize tat Cavell’s meaning can’t be meant or said in tese oter systems of tougt: tat is meaning won’t “fit” tose contexts as a matter of logic. Te sleigt of and amounts to imagining tat after one as extracted a sign out of one context of use, tat te sign someow magically retains its meaning, and ten discovering tat, lo and beold, it can’t retain tis original meaning in a substantially different context of use. Suc a maneuver could tempt us to conclude someting like “te Yukagir understanding of self is incommensurable wit te Western understanding”. But tat would involve overlooking tat as soon as a lexical item is removed from te original context of use and left in limbo as a bare sign, tere was no more “meaning” remaining for to fit or not to fit into te new and supposedly incommensurable context. Te same signs in te new context may mean watever tey mean, and tis may indeed be different from wat tose signs mean in te first context. But tis is not te same as te first meaning not fitting into te slot occupied by te second meaning. All I am doing, so tis potential criticism goes, is identifying some superficial syntactical or lexical similarities, te occurrences of te same signs in different contexts, and drawing tendentious pilosopical conclusions about wat can and cannot be tougt in tose contexts. To claim terefore tat Cavell can’t articulate is skepticism witin te logical parameters provided by te logic of affect tat eac of tese oter cultures exibit, is not just a bold metapysical claim, it is downrigt incoerent. Claiming tat “Cavell can’t say tat” logically only works if I can provide some story about wat “tat” is supposed to be. And, as we ave just seen, it is in te very nature of tis trick as I’ve described it tat no suc story is being told. My attempt to sow tat Cavellian skepticism cannot be formulated by drawing te limits around wat can be said in tese oter tougt systems, so tis line of criticism migt go, is simply unintelligible.175 But in fact, I am not relying on anyting like an incommensurability tesis and I am not claiming tat Cavellian skepticism cannot, as a matter of logic, be formulated witin tese oter “conceptual scemes” or systems of tougt. I am only claiming tat I, for one, cannot make sense of wat it would mean for te Yukagirs to sense (or repress) te “brute metapysical fact” of teir separateness given wat Willerslev reports. But I am also claiming tat I doubt anyone else can make sense of tis eiter.176 Tere is noting logically amiss ere. More importantly, it is essential to reject an assumption on wic te accusation of my relying on an incommensurability tesis may rest in te first place. Tis assumption is tat I am treating tese accounts as toug tey consisted of uninterpreted strings of signs and ten drawing my conclusion. Tis is false. On te contrary, I take te etnograpies and istories in wic tese accounts appear to contain careful translations, not merely unusual strings of signs. I take it tat istorians and etnograpers (and opefully teir attentive readers) actually ave some measure of compreension of tese strange pronouncements, even if tey do not sit easily wit our ordinary understanding. Addressing tis issue, Stratern writes, It is important to sow tat inapplicability is not just a result of poor translation. Our own metapors reflect a deeply rooted metapysics wit manifestations tat surface in all kinds of analyses.177 At te same time we may not be justified in assuming tat suc translations are based on scant evidence, on mere individual occurrences of expressions taken out of context, so tat a more extensive comparison would surely remove for us any oddity. Stratern writes in tis vein, “Comparative analysis does not in te end turn on te applicability of single concepts but on te comparison of wole systems”.178 Consider in tis ligt te following passage from J. Prytz Joansen’s work in New Zealand: According to our knowledge te reason wy te Ngatiwatuwa came to Kaipara was a murder committed by te Ngatikaumateika. Tis tribe murdered my ancestor Taureka. Te tribe lived in Hokianga. Tis country was teirs, te tribe’s. My ome was Muriwenua, it was my permanent residence because my ancestor lived tere. Later I left Muriwenua because of tis murder. Ten I tried to revenge myself, and Hokianga’s people were defeated and I took possession of te old country. Because of tis battle te wole of Hokianga was finally taken by me rigt to Maunganui, and I lived in te country because all te people ad been killed.179 Joansen adds almost laconically, “All te events described … took place long before te narrator was born”.180 If after aving learned teir informant’s language and conducted extensive field work, investigators found tat, given te perplexity tey cause, suc statements could be rendered wit more tame translations, tey would ave no good reason for not providing tose instead of te translations one so frequently encounters in teir works. Tat is, unless we are to accuse a efty portion of etnograpers of rank sensationalism, we will ave to regard passages like te previous one as real attempts to give meaningful interpretations tat express some understanding. (I esitate to write “express at least partial understanding” because tat implies tat we ave some working notion of wat complete understanding looks like, wic, unless we aren’t concerned about being dogmatic, we sould admit we do not). If we were to look askance at etnograpies and istories for containing paradoxical descriptions, ten te only occurrences of sentences like, “unlike people in X, te people in Y, tink Z”, would be te ones we could easily domesticate. Having little or no knowledge of German beer or religious geograpy would not present muc of an obstacle to assimilating a sentence suc as “unlike Düsseldorfers wo like Alt Bier best, te people in Køln tink Kölsc is best” to “unlike te people in St. Louis wo tink Budweiser is te best beer, te people in Milwaukee tink Miller is te best beer”. If tese were te only cases, tere wouldn’t be muc call for etnograpies at all (and maybe not muc call for imported beer). I am not, terefore, arguing tat we cannot as a matter of logic armonize wat certain peoples are reported as saying about teir lives and ow Cavell as construed our predicament. I am arguing rater tat, as a matter of fact, I (we) don’t know ow to reconcile te Cavellian picture wit tese (and I suspect countless oter) cases of wat oter peoples say or ave said about temselves. One could of course find oneself driven (or tempted?) to conclude tat rater tan regarding certain oter societies as aving a vastly different concept of te self, we sould say tat it is simply not clear ow tey can be regarded as aving suc a concept of self at all. I tink tere are reasons for resisting suc a move, but in any case, it offers no elp to te Cavellian picture, resting as tat does on Cavell’s essentialist claim about te ubiquity of skepticism and its originating experience.181 VIII Even if my arguments in te previous section sould allay certain logical suspicions about my istoricist-oriented critique of Cavellian skepticism, tere are furter concerns tat I need to address. Tese pertain to my use of istorical and antropological texts in my critique of Cavell. First, it migt be argued tat I ave framed te issue wrong and ave distorted te nature of Cavell’s main claim about wat works suc as Otello are meant to reveal about te uman condition. At te beginning of Part V, I provided a defense of my attributing to Cavell a kind of antropology. Tis was directed in te first instance at tose wit an empirical bent of mind wo migt resist or even resent tis word being used to describe a tinker wose conclusions were primarily drawn from te arts. I can imagine, owever, owls of protest coming from oter quarters to te effect tat my critique of Cavell, drawing as it does on istory and antropology, completely misses its mark precisely because it construes im as making someting like a universal empirical claim. Te complaint migt run like tis: Wat you are in effect accusing Cavell of doing is making a asty generalization. You are, tat is, saying tat e draws a false empirical generalization about te uman condition from an inadequate data set. But tat is not at all te kind of reasoning in wic Cavell is engaged. He is taking imself and caracters suc as Lear and Otello as representatives of umanity, in te way in wic an etologist migt say “te Canada lynx as long ear tufts, flared facial ruff, and sort, bobbed tail wit a completely black tip. It as unusually large paws tat act like snowsoes in very deep snow, tick fur and long legs and feeds primarily on te snowsoe are”. It would ardly be to te point, tis line of counter-argument would continue, for you to assert tat tis statement was false because I ad found a Canada lynx wo didn’t eat meat. Tat would misunderstand te nature of te statement, wic describes wat is typical or representative of te Canada lynx. My attempt to refute te statement by pointing to our supposed vegetarian would merely reveal tat I did not understand te grammar of suc statements in te first place.182 If tis were so, ten I would be in full agreement wit tis argument. In fact, Geertz makes tis very same point: Te notion tat unless a cultural penomenon is empirically universal it cannot reflect anyting about te nature of man is about as logical as te notion tat because sickle-cell anemia if, fortunately, not universal, it cannot tell us anyting about uman genetic processes.183 Well and good. I am not, owever, accusing Cavell of making a asty generalization, but rater of confusing a figure as representative for umanity wo is rater better tougt of as representative of one culture. If lots of our vegetarian lynxes start sowing up in peoples’ vegetable patces in Alberta, we would ave ample reason to retink te wort of te etologist’s original claim about lynx diet, owever we wis to regard its grammatical status. Claims of wat is typical of tis or tat kind of living creature are, to be sure, not te same as universal generalizations. But neiter are tey immune against observable facts. More seriously, I migt be accused of selectively coosing my sources and so stacking te deck against Cavell’s views. More specifically, someone migt accuse me of presuming tat Taylor can be employed against Cavell as “te voice of intellectual istory” on te one and, wile Willerslev and oters are called into service as te “voices of antropology” on te oter. Wereas in trut, no one could responsibly pretend tat a single or small subset of tinkers can simply be taken as speaking finally and autoritatively for teir respective fields on a subject as vast as te nature of te self. Tis criticism could even be extended if someone wised to accuse me of taking myself to be te autoritative voice of tese voices, tereby making a blunt instrument even blunter. And it is true tat istorical and antropological data sould not be cerry picked and urled at one’s opponents, as muc as tat image appeals to me, and despite te fact tat I believe pilosopers sometimes deserve suc treatment. But I am not assuming in any way tat Taylor and Willserslev et al. are te only important voices ere or tat I am teir only reliable reporter. Tey are all merely individual (albeit very pertinent) voices and my take on teir takes is just tat. It remains te case, owever, tat tese works are part of te record, and granting for te moment tat tey ave been competently researced and written and tat my renderings are fair and balanced, tey present a significant callenge to Cavell’s viewpoint. After all, since Cavell is te one making gestures at typicality, te burden of argument is on im. I merely ave to play defense. But not only do I not presume to be te sole legitimate voice for te antropological and istorical material sketced previously, I sould also make clear tat despite my reliance on is work, Willerslev and I may be in substantial disagreement about an important aspect of is findings. Recall tat in Part V I quoted a passage from a well-known paper in wic Clifford Geertz asserted te peculiarity of te Western conception of te self. Willerslev appears to take deep exception to te sort of view e sees Geertz as representing. He writes, My point is not tat we sould distinguis between two different notions of te person, a Western one and a Yukagir one. Te antropological literature is crammed wit typologies of tis kind tat crudely contrast te firm self-oter boundary of te Western person wit te fluid self-oter boundary of te non-Western one. In my view, owever, suc bipolar types of personood, even if conceived as ideal types, are widely overdrawn …184 Tis passage contains a footnote wit a reference to an article by Melford Spiro tat explicitly attacks wat is taken as te Geertzian view, an attack wic I tus take Willerslev to endorse.185 Taking a brief look at Spiro’s paper provides me wit an additional opportunity for bringing out wat I see as at stake in pilosopical discussions of te self. (It will also make it more probable tat Geertz is more pilosopically sopisticated tan most of is critics would ave it.) Spiro reveals tat e was first stimulated to write in opposition to Geertz’s view not by Geertz’s original paper itself, but by two social psycologists, Markus and Kitayama (1991), wo follow im.186 Remarking on teir agreement wit Geertz’s contention tat te Western conception of te person is a “rater peculiar idea witin te context of te world’s cultures”, Spiro asserts, Tis statement, and teir approval of it, struck me as strange, for, seeing tat te person (wic Markus and Kitayama, like Geertz, conflate wit te self) as ardly been a focus of antropological inquiry, it is not inaccurate to say tat te person or self as been studied in only a small fraction of uman societies.187 He soon adds, To claim, owever, tat te Western conception of te self is different from tat of te Indian or te Japanese or te Balinese conception is one ting, but to claim tat in te context of te world cultures, te Western conception is “peculiar” is anoter, for te latter claim implies tat typologically tere are two conceptions of te self-a Western type, on te one and, and a second type tat is instantiated in all oter societies.188 Tere seem to be several complaints ere, wic it will be useful to disentangle. First, tere is Spiro’s point tat Geertz and is followers ave failed to distinguis between te concept of te person and te concept of te self. Second, tere is te claim tat te concept of te person as simply received too little attention in te literature for te Geertzian view to ave been validated. Tird, tere is an objection tat te researcers wom Spiro criticizes ave taken on an eiter/or typology of Western/non-Western as te only legitimate axis of comparison for understanding various cultural conceptions of te self (and/or person). And fourt, altoug Spiro doesn’t mention it explicitly in te passages just cited but does devote a fair amount of attention to it in is paper, e argues tat as an empirical matter, Geertz’s talk of a “Western conception of te person as a bounded, unique, more or less integrated motivational and cognitive universe” just isn’t tat peculiar after all. Next, I will try to address all but te second objection.189 To begin wit, I will take up Spiro’s and Willerslev’s tird complaint, tat te very talk of a Western/non-Western typology, or wat for my purposes ere I am taking to be te same, a buffered/non-buffered typology, is someow illegitimate. Spiro asserts tat, in my view a typology of te self and/or its cultural conception wic consists of only two types, a Western and a non-Western, even if conceived as ideal types, is muc too restrictive. Surely, some non-Western selves, at least, are as different from one anoter as eac, in turn, is different from any Western self.190 “Ideal type” is of course Max Weber’s term of art. We will revisit it later wen I discuss Spiro’s use of “cultural conception”, wic I take to be more or less equivalent. For now, I merely want to point out tat I believe tat Willerslev and Spiro are at te very least uncaritable and quite likely just wrong in teir complaint on tis point. Let’s assume for a moment tat tere were good reasons for believing tat “te Western, buffered” self really was in some sense unique, or at least atypical, so tat a social scientist could wit a relatively clean conscience assert tat “Westerners are pretty muc like tis (buffered), wile (most of) ‘te rest’ are not”. And let’s assume, moreover, tat it is true as Willerslev asserts tat muc attention in te literature ad been devoted to making te case tat tis is so. Wile it migt lead us to ask wy suc a finding (if it is one) bared repeated pointing out, tis alone would not necessarily make it irrelevant to empasize it in any particular case. In fact, if tis purported difference as received so muc attention, tis may be because it is considered by many researcers to be ugely important tat one understand its istory, contingency, and significance. But my main point ere in responding to tis objection is tat te buffered/non-buffered dicotomy is of course only one axis of comparison and no one (and not Geertz) as so far as I can tell claimed tat it is te sole legitimate caracteristic to investigate. Tere are likely countless comparisons tat can and ave been employed by researcers, wic lend support to diverse sorts of groupings. Some comparisons may rely on te same kind of eiter/or conceptual bifurcated structure as te buffered/non-buffered distinction does. Oters may wis to avoid suc sarp dicotomies and see fit to use a muc more graded conceptual scale for te purposes of te comparison one wants to make. Te upsot is tat so long as no pernicious dogmas lie at teir basis, tere is no call for te writings of Geertz et al. to raise any unsavory suspicions about metodological framework. In order to address te first and fourt objections, it will elp if I give a sort outline of Spiro’s own conceptual sceme for tinking about te questions at and. Spiro’s arguments rest in large part on is reliance on tree ideas and is claims tat te work of te social scientists e criticizes fails to respect te important distinctions between tem. Te tree ideas are tat of “cultural conception of te person”, “self-presentation”, and “self-representation”. According to Spiro, te cultural conception of te person is an ideal type of understanding of wat a self is based on suc generally sared public, cultural factors suc as morals, religion, familiar artistic works, knowledge of social structure, ideology, etc. He notes first tat, some investigators ascertain te caracteristics of te “self” and/or its cultural conception as an inference from classical texts, some from cultural symbols, some from beavioral observations, some by means of eliciting procedures, and so on.191 Subsequently e remarks tat typically, antropologists (and comparative social psycologists) do not investigate te self or te individual’s conception of is self (te self-representation), but te cultural conception of te person. Tey mostly arrive at tis conception by investigating some set of cultural symbols of a social group, from wic tey infer its cultural conception of te person, altoug in a few instances tey do so by means of various experimental tasks.192 Wile Spiro does not accuse tose social scientists wom e tinks traffic merely in cultural conceptions of te person as conducting teir researc entirely from te comfort of teir libraries, and wile e does not imply tat te cultural conception of te person (or any oter ideal type) is witout some social scientific value, e tinks tat to settle for suc a generality for one’s understanding of te self is to settle far too ceaply. It is difficult to clarify te next idea, self-presentation, witout already drawing on ideas from te cultural conception, because troug language self-presentation already interfaces wit te cultural conception. In a roug sense, we can say tat according to Spiro, self-presentation is someting like an individual’s conscious, conceptual take on erself as well as te self-articulation tat se more or less consciously presents to oters. If te social scientist boters at all to conduct one on one interviews, ten Spiro would likely say tat it is te self-presentation se is likely to encounter, a kind of presentation tat is usually deceptively sot troug wit te expected cultural generalities caracteristic of te cultural conception. Te true self, in suc cases, is obscured. Spiro doesn’t provide muc independent description in is paper about te level e terms “self-representation” (naturally), but it is clear tat it is at tis fundamentally individual bio-psycological, affective, and largely unconscious level were te real investigative work ougt to focus. Suffice to say, e tinks tis all-important level escapes te likes of Geertz and is co-interpretivists, stuck as tey are at te levels of te purely symbolic. He complains tat most of tese studies assume tat cultural conceptions of te person are isomorpic wit te actors’ conceptions of te self, and some also assume tat tey are isomorpic wit te actors’ mental representations of teir self, and wit teir self itself.193 His explanation for suc negligence is tat many antropologists remain unfortunately committed to te regnant teory of wolesale cultural determinism, tey take it as self-evident … tat cultural conceptions of te self are isomorpic not only wit te actors’ conception of te self, but wit teir mental representation of teir own self as well.194 Presumably wat Spiro means by “wolesale cultural determinism” is someting like a quasi-mecanical top-down determination not only of te ways in wic actors present temselves to te public (including temselves) but also a cultural colonizing of teir deepest private somatic sense of temselves. On tis basis Spiro concludes tat aving learned the hard way that one cannot validly infer actors’ conception of the self, let alone their mental representations of their own self, from the normative cultural conception, it is not surprising that was rather skeptical of Geertz’s claim that from a study of symbolic cultural forms alone, one can validly infer the manner in which people “actually represent themselves to themselves.”195 Based on the distinctions between the cultural conception of the self (more or less identical with the concept of a person for Spiro), self-presentation, and self-representation, Spiro is able to lay out some specific objections against “wholesale cultural determinism”. With regard to Markus and Kitayama, Spiro maintains that their statement that in non-Western societies “others are included within the boundaries of the self” would mean that an individual’s other-representations are located within his self-representation, and such a condition (according to modern psychiatry) is a sign of rather severe psychopathology.196 Spiro elaborates a bit further on: f for Markus and Kitayama the term “self” denotes (as I think is most likely the case) some psychological entity (an ego, a soul, an “I”) within the person, and “others” denotes such an entity within other persons, then non-Western peoples, for whom “others” are allegedly included within the boundaries of the self, would be characterized by little, if any, self-other differentiation, and like William James and . I. Hallowell, I find such a notion very difficult to comprehend. Thus, both James and Hallowell, respectively the preeminent psychological and anthropological (cross-cultural) theorists of the self (in my view at any rate), construe self-other differentiation—the sense that one’s self, or one’s own person, is bounded, or separate from all other persons—as a distinguishing feature of the very notion of human nature.197 On this point, Spiro thus concludes as follows: lthough Geertz contends that in his studies of Bali, Java, and Morocco he has employed “symbolic forms” to describe the actors’ own “sense of self” … , in my judgment he has employed them to describe the ways in which actors are often designated and identified by others, and in which they often present themselves to others. In other words, although he has described the actors’ self-presentation, he has not described their “sense of self,” or their self-representation.198 In addition to the previous arguments based mainly on purportedly neglected conceptual distinctions, Spiro also makes recourse to research conducted in Bali by Unni Wikan that he believes empirically refutes Geertz’s views, in particular by showing up his more dramatic claims such as that in Balinese social life “It is dramatis personae, not actors, that endure; indeed, it is dramatis personae, not actors, that in the proper sense really exist”.199 few of the offending passages from Geertz’s earlier writings on Balinese culture provide some vivid examples of the ideas that Spiro sees Wikan’s research as refuting. In one passage, Geertz claims that The various “village chiefs” and “folk priests” on the Sudra level, and, on the Triwangas, the hosts of “kings,” “princes,” “lords,” and “high priests” do not merely occupy a role. They become, in the eyes of themselves and those around them, absorbed into it. They are truly public men, men for whom other aspects of personhood – individual character, birth order, kinship relations, procreative status, and prestige rank take, symbolically at least, a secondary position. We, focusing upon psychological traits as the heart of personal identity, would say they have sacrificed their true selves to their role; they, focusing on social position, say that their role is of the essence of their true selves.200 few pages on, we find much the same idea: The nearly faceless, thoroughly conventionalized, never-changing icons by which nameless gods known only by their public titles are, year after year, represented in the thousands of temple festivals across the island comprise the purest expression of the Balinese concept of personhood. Genuflecting to them (or, more precisely, to the gods for the moment resident in them) the Balinese are not just acknowledging divine power. They are also confronting the image of what they consider themselves at bottom to be; an image which the biological, psychological, and sociological concomitants of being alive, the mere materialities of historical time, tend only to obscure from site.201 Geertz sums up his interpretation with the terse assertion: “The illuminating paradox of Balinese formulations of personhood is that they are – in our terms anyway – depersonalizing”.202 ccording to Spiro, Wikan’s work shows that the Balinese, pace Geertz, are not merely or even primarily concerned with a cultural conception of the person or in the public personae the cultural conception extracts from them in their self-presentation, but instead are centrally occupied with their individual emotional states in a way that indicates they experience their inner lives as every bit as much of a bounded, unique, more or less integrated motivational and cognitive universe as does the typical Westerner. Spiro claims that Wikan’s more psychologically focused investigations reveal that the Balinese have a constant concern with health, which is hardly surprising given that half of all deaths are attributed either to black magic perpetrated by “intimate others,” or to poison administered by them. Moreover, for the Balinese, she writes, the self is experienced as “steadily exposed to myriad health-endangering forces… . Fellow beings, deities, demons, sundry (super) natural spirits, and the souls of the dead can strike a person sick or dead”. gain, “the Balinese live in a world where murder or attempted murder resulting in sickness from sorcery is the order of the day”.203 He adds later, Thus, Balinese actors fear their own anger, according to Wikan, because of their belief that should they offend others, the latter will use sorcery and poison to make them ill, and they will suffer supernatural punishment, as well.204 ccording to Spiro, these considerations show that the Balinese are as individualistic in their self-presentation as the supposedly peculiar Westerner. In fact, in a twist towards the end of his paper, Spiro contends that not only is the allegedly peculiar Western self not peculiar within the context of world cultures, it is nothing but a mere “straw man” even within the context of the West itself: “No major Western student of the self, none at any rate that I am aware of, holds the view that the self, Western or otherwise, is not interdependent.205 Spiro’s conclusion is thus that there is evidence for the proposition that many putative characteristics of the Western self, which allegedly make it “peculiar,” are to a greater or lesser degree also found in the non-Western self; conversely, many putative characteristics of the non-Western self, which allegedly are its distinguishing features, are to a greater or lesser degree also found in the Western self.206 In response to Spiro’s claim that a failure to respect conceptual distinctions accounts for a large part of the error he attributes to Geertz and others, I would argue that while it is doubtless a mistake to think that one can simply read off what Spiro calls “self-presentation” and “self-representation” from cultural conceptions, it is also dubious in the extreme to believe that these three categorizations can be somehow understood in conceptual isolation from one another. For example, the very idea that self-representation can be made intelligible in logical isolation from the shared concepts of language, and so the cultural conception, in which each individual partakes, is a fantasy. Presumably even a psychologically focused investigation into an individual’s sense of herself, her self-representation, one that aims to bypass what she might be inclined to share, her self-presentation, is going to have to make use of concepts drawn from a shared language. nd it’s hard to see how this will not involve relying on concepts connected to the cultural conception of the person. Without being isomorphic, concepts taken from the language of the culture, the concepts I rely on in my conscious modes of self-interpretation (my self-presentation), and my own sense of myself (my self-representation) are bound to interpenetrate each other. What is the alternative? Is it the idea that we can somehow make sense of a language of thought, a private language as it were, that each individual speaks to herself and that exists in logical isolation from the public language she shares with others? Is the suggestion that anthropologists practice mind reading? s for Spiro’s assertion that the experience of a kind of non-buffered interpenetration of self and other ascribed to various non-Western peoples by some social scientists is better described as an implausible inadvertent ascription of mass psychopathology, this is contested by Willerslev’s and Viveiros de Castro’s descriptions of a central aspect of Yukaghir and merindian animism respectively, namely, the persistently felt need to distinguish oneself from the animal world so that one does not simply vanish into a fusion of selves. Neither Willerslev nor Viveiros de Castro feel tempted to offer a psychiatric interpretation of their informants. Spiro reports that he finds such things difficult to understand; I can certainly agree with that. Yet I would have thought that such a sense of difficulty would be a place for anthropological reflection to begin rather than a place for it to stop.207 Next, Spiro’s employment of Wikan’s work in Bali fails badly as a critique of Geertz. The cited examples, far from exhibiting an inner sense of individual independence, actually illustrate how the cultural conception of a person in Bali, including the idea that “intimate others” are capable of inflicting evil on one’s self, actually interpenetrates people’s inner self-representation of who they are. Indeed, it does so in a way that flatly contradicts the idea that self-representation and cultural conception are sharply differentiated in this case. If Wikan’s descriptions are reliable, the “others” seem to reach right to the core of the self. Rather than providing a non-Western example of a bounded, buffered self, the examples show precisely the opposite. The Javanese fear of the effects of both black magic from without and the effects of negative emotions on others from within read to me like textbook descriptions of porosity. The specific ideational content of such fears reveals a subject’s sense of himself as non-buffered, not bounded, and dependent.208 Finally, there is Spiro’s assertion that no major Western student of the self ever held the view that the self is not interdependent. Except for William James and .I. Hallowell, I am not certain whom Spiro counts as a major student of the self. Presumably Spiro would exclude Luther, Calvin, Descartes, Pascal, and Kant, who despite their substantial contributions to our present self-understanding chose to disregard the obvious empirical facts of human development, viz. that each of us is born biologically and psychologically helpless and must receive enough care in our earliest years to make responsible adulthood possible. What their contributions to the peculiar modern view stem from, however, is precisely, for better or worse, their not stopping at these facts, but instead offering their articulations of an individualistic conception of the self that is shaped by the requirements of a philosophical view of human agency and cognition. However much this view sits in tension with a more interactionist perspective, it has clearly had enormous historical significance for how people have regarded themselves in the modern West. In the end, whether Geertz is correct to declare the Western conception of the self to be a “peculiar thing” is largely an empirical matter. So far as I know, the buffered self as it is designated in modern Western epistemology has only found mass instantiation once.209 The key elements of that conception, as one sees perhaps most clearly in Descartes, are that of a morally independent integrated motivational and cognitive universe. Crucially, they entail a view of the self as normatively and logically distinct from the “external world”. Secondary qualities, moral “properties”, and rationality itself are collapsed into the self, which thus gazes out on a completely disenchanted world of objects. While what Hume said of skepticism may also be true of this sense of isolation from the world, that no sooner does one leave one’s room and engage in practical activity than it becomes psychologically remote, the sense of the world as epistemologically remote and disenchanted is still educated common sense in much of the contemporary West. (In certain circles, even to question the subject/object divide is to run the risk being accused of trafficking in hocus pocus.) Far from aligning with the depiction of the Western conception offered by Geertz and others, the Balinese examples Spiro cites against it serve instead to reveal people who understand themselves as living in direct epistemological, semantic, moral, and causal contact with a “normatively structured” world. If Taylor is roughly right, then not long after the Protestant Reformation and the rise of capitalism not only the lifeworld of intellectuals, but much of everyday life in Europe was undergoing systematic disenchantment in a manner that would have rendered it nearly impossible to understand in the terms Wikan finds apt in modern Bali. The kind of pietism described by Weber was not just for the scientifically or philosophically educated, but characterizes a worldview inhabited by the more broadly educated public. For the educated classes in Europe and North merica, the scientific revolution followed by the Enlightenment put the final touches on the process of banishing both God and human mental life from nature. This is what I take Geertz to be referring to in the quotation at issue; I suspect it is pretty peculiar. IX The main reason for my extended discussions of historical and ethnographic material was to make it more plausible that Cavell’s view of our essential human condition as one of separateness is in effect an ethnocentric dogma, albeit perhaps a tacit one. In addition, however, my discussion also touches indirectly on a closely related issue that goes to the heart of Cavell’s vision of language. I will briefly take this up now. s we saw earlier, Cavell treats the possibility of skepticism as essentially tied to his insight that criteria are not self-applying, that they need to be projected into a context of use. This point is at the center of his argument that the skeptic’s procedure, which is launched by claims such as “here is a hand” or “here is a piece of wax”, involves the assumption that these utterances have specific significance despite their being uttered in a “non-claim” context. In other words, the criteria guiding the use of these expressions are taken by the skeptic to be magically self-applying, an idea Cavell rightly finds unintelligible. In order to be projected into a context, language thus requires the correlative concept of a language user, a particular kind of agent. This requirement for projection in a context, moreover, is internal to the contingency of language, in particular to its teaching and learning, but also to the inevitable failures and mistakes in our projections. It is the intrinsic difficulty we humans experience living with this essential contingency that generates skepticism. The very idea of the consequences of such failures, let alone their actual occasions, exacerbate our latent sense of being locked out that these failures make manifest. Given that the criteria we project are our distinctly human way of reaching the world and reaching each other, it seems understandable that the naked isolation that such failure reveals, especially in a best-case scenario, could prove terrifying. But this issue of linguistic agency may appear to raise a serious problem for my claims that there are no compelling reasons for thinking that Cavell’s understanding of the self is universally valid. If his specific ontology is actually necessary for the very concept of linguistic agency, we would be faced with some unattractive options in light of the anthropological material I have discussed. In other words, we would be faced with a question as to how, given what many ethnographers report, their informants’ statements could be the expressions of language users at all. “Logically”, I suppose, we could infer that, lacking the required agency, these people are not language users at all. But not only is this option absurd, it is belied by the fact that self-descriptions indicating a non-buffered self pervade the historical and anthropological record.
